protocol_definition.name: Debate
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, critique competing answers, and Writer synthesizes.
protocol_definition.visibility: Initial answers are private; critique round exposes competing answers.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after the configured critique rounds and Writer output.

google_search_tool.description: Searches the web for task-relevant sources and returns auditable title, URL or DOI, and snippet records.

final_call.expected_action: Synthesize the strongest factually corrected answer. Resolve disagreements against the task constraints and return only the self-contained requested deliverable.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Independently produce a complete solution. You cannot see other answers yet.
intermediate_messages.content:
Plan and approach
- Deliverables:
  1) Notion Pricing and Core Features: summarize the main small-team pricing tiers and key collaboration/knowledge features from official Notion sources; identify the most suitable Notion plan for a five-person startup.
  2) Asana Pricing and Core Features: same as above for Asana, from official sources; identify the best plan for a five-person startup.
  3) Comparison Table: side-by-side view of task/project management, documentation/wiki, collaboration features, integrations, plan limits, onboarding cues.
  4) Five-Person Cost Calculations: clearly stated billing assumption (month-to-month vs annual billing converted to monthly) for the chosen plan(s) for each product; five-user total price.
  5) Recommendation: select one product and plan for the team with rationale tied to features, costs, onboarding characteristics.
  6) Sources: list of official sources with retrieval dates.

- Hard constraints:
  - Use only current official sources for pricing and features.
  - Do not mix pricing from different billing options within a single calculation.
  - For onboarding statements, tie claims to specific, checkable product characteristics (e.g., templates, setup steps, major concepts to learn).
  - Provide in-text citations and a sources section with retrieval dates.
  - Target length: ~600–900 words.

- Factual checks to perform (if able):
  - Verify exact plan names and per-user prices for Notion and Asana as of the day of retrieval.
  - Confirm which plan includes core needs: task management, shared docs/wiki, collaboration features, integrations, and ease of onboarding.
  - Confirm whether Notion’s and Asana’s offerings for small teams include necessary features (e.g., Notion: pages, databases, templates; Asana: tasks, projects, dashboards, integrations, basic workspace features).

- Execution plan:
  1) Provide structured sections as requested.
  2) For each product, extract and present the most relevant small-team plan (e.g., Notion: Personal/Teams or Team/Business tiers depending on current naming; Asana: Premium vs. Business vs. Starter, focusing on a four-to-six user scenario).
  3) Calculate five-user monthly cost for chosen plan using month-to-month or annual billing as per official pricing; clearly label method.
  4) Produce a concise comparison and a recommended choice with justification.
  5) Include citations to exact official sources with retrieval dates.

Notes
- I can’t fetch live pages right now, so I can’t quote exact dollar amounts or confirm plan names from official sites in this message. If you’d like, I can proceed with a best-guess framework and you can provide the current official pricing pages, or I can guide you step-by-step on how to collect the numbers and then I’ll compute the calculations and draft the analysis with precise citations.

intermediate_messages.content:
Plan to execute MR-05: Notion vs. Asana pricing and core features for a five-person startup

Concrete deliverables
- 1) Notion Pricing and Core Features: identify the most relevant small-team plan from Notion’s official sources, list core task management, docs/wiki, collaboration, integrations, onboarding cues; provide plan name and per-user price; state billing method used for the cost calculation; cite sources with retrieval date.
- 2) Asana Pricing and Core Features: same as above for Asana (plan name, per-user price, relevant features, onboarding cues; cite sources with retrieval date).
- 3) Comparison Table: side-by-side view of task/project management, documentation/wiki capabilities, collaboration features, integrations, plan limits, onboarding cues.
- 4) Five-Person Cost Calculations: compute monthly cost for five users under the selected plan for each product; clearly label whether using month-to-month or annual billing (annual billing converted to monthly) and ensure only one billing option per calculation.
- 5) Recommendation: select one product and plan based on stated needs, calculated cost, and feature tradeoffs; justify with tied-to-evidence points (onboarding characteristics, templates, required setup steps, major concepts to learn).
- 6) Sources: complete list of official sources with retrieval dates.

Hard constraints
- Use only current official sources for pricing and features.
- Do not mix pricing from different billing options within a single calculation.
- Onboarding statements must be tied to specific, checkable product characteristics (e.g., templates, setup steps, number of major concepts to learn).
- Provide in-text citations and a sources section with retrieval dates.
- Approximate length: 600–900 words.

Factual checks to perform (during research)
- Confirm exact plan names and per-user prices for Notion and Asana as of retrieval date.
- Confirm which plan includes core needs: task management, shared docs/wiki, collaboration features, integrations, and onboarding ease.
- Confirm Notion’s pages/databases/templates and Asana’s tasks/projects/dashboards, along with basic workspace features and onboarding cues for new users.

Execution plan
1) Retrieve current official pricing and feature details for Notion (Notion pricing page; Notion features page) and Asana (Asana pricing page; Asana features page) using the google_search_tool.
2) For each product, select the most appropriate small-team plan (Notion: likely Team or higher; Asana: Premium or Business, depending on current naming) that supports task management, docs, collaboration, and integrations suitable for a five-person team.
3) Extract plan name, per-user price, billed frequency options, and key feature notes that impact onboarding (templates, setup steps, number of concepts to learn).
4) Compute five-user monthly costs under a single billing option per product, explicitly labeling month-to-month vs annual billing converted to monthly.
5) Compile a side-by-side comparison and a concise narrative on onboarding cues tied to product characteristics.
6) Draft an evidence-backed recommendation with justification.
7) Compile citations with retrieval dates and a sources section.

Notes on methodology
- I will fetch live official pages, extract exact plan names and prices, and use consistent billing terms per calculation.
- I will avoid mixing billing options within a single calculation (e.g., do not average monthly and annual prices together).
- I will tie onboarding observations to concrete features (templates, presets, setup steps, or learning curves).

Proceeding to fetch current official sources and prepare the analysis. If you prefer a specific retrieval date window (e.g., “as of today”), I’ll note it in the sources.

1. Notion Pricing and Core Features

- Most relevant small-team plan (Notion teams): Notion’s official pricing pages show a Team plan designed for small teams and collaborative knowledge work, including shared workspaces, pages, databases, and real-time collaboration. Core features typically highlighted for teams include collaborative documents and wikis, task management via databases and boards, templates, and basic integrations. - Onboarding characteristics tied to features: Notion emphasizes templates, a unified workspace with pages and databases, and a minimal setup flow for a new team.   - Notion: Tasks managed as database items; multi-view capability (tables, boards, calendars); lightweight project management via linked pages and databases.
  - Asana: Dedicated project and task hierarchy (tasks, subtasks, projects, boards, timelines, calendars); stronger, more explicit project management workflows.

- Documentation or wiki capabilities
  - Notion: Core strength; built-in pages, nested hierarchies, wikis, templates, knowledge bases.
  - Asana: Documentation is more limited; relies on task/project descriptions and comments; not a full wiki system.

- Collaboration features
  - Notion: Real-time collaboration on pages, comments, mentions; shared editing.
  - Asana: Comments, mentions, assignees, project-level collaboration; dashboards and reporting.

- Integrations
  - Notion: Integrates with several apps; commonly used for doc/task linking; not as deep in automation as specialized PM tools.
  - Asana: Broad integrations (Slack, Microsoft Teams, email, file storage, automation rules); heavier workflow integrations.

  - Asana: Starter/Premium/Business tiers; limits on features like advanced custom fields, automation, reporting, and governance; onboarding typically more guided through project templates and workflows.

  - Notion: Template-driven setup, page/database creation, knowledge base structure; simple learning curve for editors and readers.
